stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} \). The graph of \( f \) is shifted to the right by [[h]] and up by [[k]] to produce the graph of \( g \); equivalently, \( g(x) = f(x - [[h]]) + [[k]] \). What is \( g([[x0]]) \)?
solution: Shifting right by [[h]] replaces \( x \) with \( x - [[h]] \), and shifting up by [[k]] adds [[k]]: \( g(x) = [[a]](x - [[h]])^{2} + [[k]] \). So \( g([[x0]]) = [[a]]([[x0]] - [[h]])^{2} + [[k]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This shifts left instead of right, using \( x + [[h]] \) rather than \( x - [[h]] \).
This shifts down instead of up, subtracting [[k]] rather than adding it.
This performs the horizontal shift but forgets the vertical shift of [[k]].
This adds [[k]] but forgets to shift the input horizontally by [[h]].
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \). The graph of \( g \) is the reflection of the graph of \( f \) across the \( x \)-axis, so \( g(x) = -f(x) \). What is \( g([[x0]]) \)?
solution: Reflecting across the \( x \)-axis negates every output: \( g(x) = -f(x) \). First evaluate \( f([[x0]]) = [[a]]([[x0]])^{2} + ([[b]])([[x0]]) + ([[c]]) \), then negate it to get \( g([[x0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to reflect at all, reporting \( f([[x0]]) \) itself.
This negates only the quadratic term instead of the whole expression.
This negates only the linear and constant terms instead of the whole expression.
This negates the quadratic and linear terms but not the constant, an inconsistent sign error.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{3} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \). The graph of \( g \) is the reflection of the graph of \( f \) across the \( y \)-axis, so \( g(x) = f(-x) \). What is \( g([[x0]]) \)?
solution: Reflecting across the \( y \)-axis replaces \( x \) with \( -x \): \( g(x) = f(-x) \). So \( g([[x0]]) = [[a]](-[[x0]])^{3} + ([[b]])(-[[x0]]) + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to reflect at all, reporting \( f([[x0]]) \) itself.
This reflects across the \( x \)-axis instead of the \( y \)-axis, negating the whole output rather than the input.
This negates \( x \) inside the cubic term but forgets to also negate it in the linear term.
This flips the sign of only the linear term instead of substituting \( -x \) throughout.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[m]])x + ([[n]]) \). The graph of \( g \) is a vertical stretch of the graph of \( f \) by a factor of [[c]], so \( g(x) = [[c]]\,f(x) \). What is \( g([[x0]]) \)?
solution: A vertical stretch by [[c]] multiplies every output by [[c]]: \( g(x) = [[c]]\,f(x) \). First \( f([[x0]]) = [[a]]([[x0]])^{2} + ([[m]])([[x0]]) + ([[n]]) \), then \( g([[x0]]) = [[c]]\cdot f([[x0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the stretch factor entirely, reporting \( f([[x0]]) \) itself.
This adds the stretch factor instead of multiplying by it.
This applies the stretch factor only to the quadratic term instead of the whole function value.
This divides by the stretch factor instead of multiplying by it (as if compressing rather than stretching).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[m]])x \). The graph of \( g \) is a horizontal compression/stretch of the graph of \( f \), so \( g(x) = f([[b]]x) \). What is \( g([[x0]]) \)?
solution: Substituting [[b]]x for x means every occurrence of \( x \) in \( f \) is replaced by \( [[b]]x \), not just the input to the leading term: \( g(x) = f([[b]]x) = [[a]]([[b]]x)^{2} + ([[m]])([[b]]x) \). So \( g([[x0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the horizontal scaling entirely, reporting \( f([[x0]]) \) itself.
This scales the whole output by [[b]] instead of scaling the input before substitution (confusing this with a vertical stretch).
This applies the scale factor only to the quadratic term's coefficient rather than to \( x \) itself.
This applies the scaling to the quadratic term but forgets to also scale \( x \) in the linear term.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: First shift the input by [[H]], then evaluate \( f \), then apply the vertical stretch [[A]] and finally add the vertical shift [[K]]: \( g([[x0]]) = [[A]]\,f([[x0]] - [[H]]) + ([[K]]) = [[answer]] \).

This forgets the horizontal shift by [[H]], evaluating \( f \) at \( [[x0]] \) directly.
This shifts left instead of right, using \( x + [[H]] \) rather than \( x - [[H]] \).

stem: The graph of \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} \) has its vertex at \( (0,0) \). The graph of \( f \) is shifted right by [[H]] and up by [[K]] to produce the graph of \( g \). What is the sum of the coordinates of the vertex of \( g \)?
solution: A horizontal shift right by [[H]] and vertical shift up by [[K]] moves the vertex from \( (0,0) \) to \( ([[H]], [[K]]) \). The sum of its coordinates is \( [[H]] + [[K]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the vertical coordinate instead of adding it.
This subtracts the horizontal coordinate instead of adding it (order reversed).
This negates the correct sum entirely, as if both shifts were in the opposite direction.
This multiplies the two coordinates instead of adding them.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{3} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \). The graph of \( g \) is obtained by rotating the graph of \( f \) by \( 180^{\circ} \) about the origin, so \( g(x) = -f(-x) \). What is \( g([[x0]]) \)?
solution: A \( 180^{\circ} \) rotation about the origin both reflects the input and negates the output: \( g(x) = -f(-x) \). So \( g([[x0]]) = -\Big([[a]](-[[x0]])^{3} + ([[b]])(-[[x0]]) + ([[c]])\Big) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets both reflections entirely, reporting \( f([[x0]]) \) itself.
This reflects across the \( x \)-axis only (negates the output) but forgets to also reflect the input.
This reflects across the \( y \)-axis only (negates the input) but forgets to also negate the output.
This negates the constant term separately instead of negating the whole expression \( f(-[[x0]]) \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
